content.full_text:
COUNTY OF SACRAMENTO
VOTER REGISTRATION & ELECTIONS
Aug 1 '91

ERNEST R. HAWKINS
REGISTRAR OF VOTERS

DWIGHT M. BEATTIE
ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
OF VOTERS

August 13, 1991


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Attention: Technical Assistance

Gentlemen:

Sacramento County Voter Registration and Elections has in-
stalled an Electronic Imaging System for the storage and
retrieval of campaign disclosure documents.

Section 81009(g) states, "After an original report or statement
or copy has been on file for at least two years, the officer
with whom it is filed may comply with this section by retaining
a copy on microfilm or other space-saving materials available
for public inspection instead of the original report or
statement or copy. Upon request, the officer shall provide
copies of such statements pursuant to Section 81008."

I am requesting that the optical disk storage system be
acknowledged as an accepted means of storage for campaign
disclosure documents.

Enclosed please find some general information about the
operation of this system.

I would very much appreciate a response in writing.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

Ernest R. Hawkins
Registrar of Voters

Enclosure


3700 BRANCH CENTER ROAD • SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95827 • (916) 366-2051
(TDD 362-2357 FAX 366-4116)

SACRAMENTO COUNTY
VOTER REGISTRATION AND ELECTIONS
3700 Branch Center Road
Sacramento, California 95827
Tel. No. (916) 366-4162


The following is an overview of the KIMS 4000 system currently being
utilized by the Campaign Disclosure Section of Sacramento County Voter
Registration and Elections.

GENERAL INFORMATION

The Kodak Electronic Imaging System (KIMS) utilizes a 650 MEGA BYTES
optical disk.

The monitor has a full size data window, views both portrait and
landscape documents.

Printer prints a copy slightly smaller than the original without
cutting off data.

The software is a 20 Megahertz (MHZ) - 386 system is menu driven,
generates requested reports, and has the ability to transfer data of
existing files.

The scanner is a flat bed. It takes approximately 2 - 3 seconds, then
another 2 - 3 seconds to appear on the screen per document; retrieval
time is approximately 2 - 3 seconds.

We have scanned the semi-annual filing documents for all of the first
half of 1991, all current and past campaign disclosure filings for
members of the Board of Supervisors, and local filings of all candidates/officeholders and local recipient committees of 1988 to
present.

The computer terminal is routinely turned on each morning and is left
operational during the day. At the end of the work day, the computer
terminal, scanner, and printer are brought down, and the optical disks
are removed and stored.

PUBLIC ACCESS

A computer terminal is provided for public access to campaign disclosure documents filed by recipient committees, candidate controlled
committees, and candidates and officeholders without committees.

The Kodak KIMS system will create an alphabetical or numerical report
of recipient committees and candidate controlled committees.

For public access, an alphabetical index of committees is provided.
The user can look up the name of any recipient committee or candidate
controlled committee to be accessed, noting the ID number.  The ID
number is then entered into the system.  The screen will show an index
of the documents filed by that committee (one at a time) in reverse
chronological filing order.  The user then scrolls through the index to
find the document(s) he wishes to either view or print.  A prompt will
appear at the bottom of the screen requesting the user to insert the
appropriate optical disk.

If the user does not find the name of candidate on the index we have
printed, he can type in the name of the candidate/officeholder.  The
KIMS system then searches by soundex--a system that identifies names by
consonant sounds.  The system will bring up a list of candidates/officeholders with similar sounding names.

SCANNING

Documents are scanned onto the system on a daily basis.  Once the
images are scanned, they are available for immediate viewing and
reproduction.

Documents can be loaded onto the automatic feeder or placed manually on
the flatbed.  The automatic feeder will take up to 50 pages at one
time.

The user has the ability to preset the scanner to recognize multiple
pages as belonging to a single document.  This feature, called Mark
Lead Page, is very helpful when it comes to indexing.  Rather than
identifying each page of a document, the user need only identify the
first page; the following pages will automatically append themselves.

When the Mark Lead Page mode is turned off, each page can be individually indexed.

At the end of each working day, we routinely backup our index to a
floppy disk and do an incremental copying of the original or source
disk (documents scanned and indexed that day) onto a target disk.  In
other words, each day every document added to the original or source
disk is added to the target disk.  (The target disk is the permanent
optical disk used for public access.)

Once a week, a tape backup is made for the entire KIMS system.  In
addition, as Optical Disks are filled to capacity, a copy is made and
stored offsite.


Prepared by Lila Sills
Election Assistant, Campaign Services
July 11, 1991
